explain.rule: Inversion means putting the auxiliary verb before the subject — the opposite of normal order. Used for: (1) Negative adverbials at the start: Never have I seen, Not only did she win, Rarely does he complain, Hardly had we arrived when... (2) Cleft sentences for emphasis: It was John who broke the window. What I need is a holiday. (3) So/such + inversion: So tired was she that she fell asleep immediately. All of these add emphasis and are common in formal English and writing.
explain.why_matters: Inversion creates emphasis and variety. It is extremely common in formal writing, journalism, speeches, and literature. Recognising and using it marks B2 competence and sophisticated style.
explain.hebrew_note: Hebrew word order is relatively flexible and can create emphasis by moving elements. English normally has fixed word order, but these inversion structures are a formal way of creating emphasis. The challenge is remembering to ALSO invert the subject and auxiliary when starting with a negative adverbial.
explain.tip: Rule for negative adverbials: when you start a sentence with Never/Rarely/Hardly/Not only/No sooner — you MUST invert subject and auxiliary: 'Never have I...' (not 'Never I have'). This is like a question structure without the question mark.

good_examples.sentence: Never have I seen such dedication.
good_examples.audio_text: Never have I seen such dedication.
good_examples.note: Inverted: Never + have + I (auxiliary before subject)

good_examples.sentence: Not only did she win the race, but she also broke the record.
good_examples.audio_text: Not only did she win the race, but she also broke the record.

good_examples.sentence: Rarely does he complain about anything.
good_examples.audio_text: Rarely does he complain about anything.

good_examples.sentence: It was John who broke the window.
good_examples.audio_text: It was John who broke the window.
good_examples.note: Cleft sentence — emphasising who did it

good_examples.sentence: What I need is a long holiday.
good_examples.audio_text: What I need is a long holiday.

good_examples.sentence: Hardly had we arrived when it started to rain.
good_examples.audio_text: Hardly had we arrived when it started to rain.

good_examples.sentence: So tired was she that she fell asleep at the table.
good_examples.audio_text: So tired was she that she fell asleep at the table.

good_examples.sentence: Under no circumstances should you share this information.
good_examples.audio_text: Under no circumstances should you share this information.

good_examples.sentence: Not until she read the email did she understand the problem.
good_examples.audio_text: Not until she read the email did she understand the problem.

good_examples.sentence: It is the quality of work that matters most.
good_examples.audio_text: It is the quality of work that matters most.

bad_examples.wrong: Never I have seen such a beautiful view.
bad_examples.correct: Never have I seen such a beautiful view.
bad_examples.reason: After negative adverbials, invert: auxiliary + subject.

bad_examples.wrong: Not only she won the race, but she also broke the record.
bad_examples.correct: Not only did she win the race, but she also broke the record.
Rarely does he complain about anything.

bad_examples.wrong: It was John that broke the window. (referring to a person)
bad_examples.correct: It was John who broke the window.
bad_examples.reason: Cleft sentences: use who for people, that for things.

bad_examples.wrong: So tired she was that she fell asleep.
bad_examples.correct: So tired was she that she fell asleep.
bad_examples.reason: So + adjective + inversion: was + she (not she was).

exercises.question: Never ___ I seen such a beautiful sunset.
exercises.hint: Negative adverbial inversion: Never + auxiliary + subject
exercises.explanation: Never have I — auxiliary before subject.

exercises.question: Not only ___ she win, but she also set a new record.
Rarely ___ he take a day off.
It was her dedication ___ made the difference.
What I really need ___ a long holiday.
exercises.explanation: What I need is — cleft with wh.

exercises.question: Hardly ___ we sat down when the phone rang.
exercises.explanation: Hardly had we — past perfect inversion.

exercises.question: So impressed ___ the judges that they gave her top marks.
Under no circumstances ___ you reveal this to anyone.
Never ___ such a talented musician.
exercises.hint: Negative adverbial inversion: Never + have + I
distractors: I have heard / I heard / have I been hearing

exercises.question: It was the manager ___ made the final decision.
Not only ___ the exam, but he also won a scholarship.
exercises.hint: Not only + did + subject + base verb
distractors: he passed / he did pass / had he passed

exercises.question: Rarely ___ I find a book I can't put down.
distractors: I do / I have / have I

exercises.question: ___ she read the letter that she finally understood.
exercises.explanation: Not until she read + did she understand.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'Never I have seen such courage.'
exercises.answer: Never have I seen such courage.
exercises.hint: After Never: invert auxiliary and subject
exercises.explanation: Never have I — auxiliary before subject.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'Not only she won, but she also broke the record.'
exercises.answer: Not only did she win, but she also broke the record.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'So tired she was that she fell asleep.'
exercises.answer: So tired was she that she fell asleep.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'It was the report that he wrote that impressed everyone.' (referring to a thing)
exercises.answer: It was the report which he wrote that impressed everyone.
exercises.hint: Cleft with a thing — that or which
exercises.explanation: It was the report which/that he wrote.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: Never have I seen a more beautiful view.
exercises.explanation: Never have I seen — auxiliary before subject.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: Not only did she win, but she also broke the record.
exercises.explanation: Not only did she + but she also.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: It was her dedication that made the difference.

speak.sentence: Never have I seen such an impressive performance in all my years of teaching.
chunks: Never have I seen such an impressive performance
in all my years of teaching.

speak.sentence: Not only did she arrive late, but she also forgot all her materials.
chunks: Not only did she arrive late,
but she also forgot all her materials.

speak.sentence: It was her passion for the subject that made her such an inspiring teacher.
chunks: It was her passion for the subject
that made her such an inspiring teacher.

speak.sentence: Rarely does a day go by without some new development in this story.
chunks: Rarely does a day go by
without some new development in this story.

speak.sentence: What I find most extraordinary is how quickly she adapted to the role.
chunks: is how quickly she adapted to the role.

speak.sentence: Hardly had they begun the meeting when the fire alarm went off.
chunks: Hardly had they begun the meeting
when the fire alarm went off.

speak.sentence: Under no circumstances should you give your password to anyone.
chunks: Under no circumstances should you give your password

speak.sentence: So difficult was the exam that only five percent of students passed.
chunks: that only five percent of students passed.
